original_abstract: Modern machine learning research is often hindered by a lack of reproducibility and the inability to effectively build upon prior experimental results. While traditional publications summarize findings in static tables and graphs, the underlying data, complex code, and fine-grained evaluation metrics remain largely inaccessible to the broader community. In this paper, we introduce OpenML, a collaborative online platform designed to transition machine learning toward a networked science model. OpenML creates a structured information commons where researchers can automatically share datasets, implementation workflows (flows), and detailed experimental results (runs). By integrating directly with popular data mining environments such as Weka, R, MOA, and RapidMiner, the platform allows scientists to contribute and organize observations with minimal effort. We demonstrate how this ecosystem fosters "designed serendipity" and a "dynamic division of labor," enabling the community to tackle harder problems, perform large-scale meta-learning studies, and automate the comparison of new algorithms against the state of the art. Finally, we discuss how OpenML provides significant benefits to individual researchers, students, and practitioners by increasing research visibility, providing new impact metrics, and establishing a collective "working memory" for the field.
humanized_abstract: Reproducibility in machine learning is a major obstacle in many current research endeavors. In addition, it is hard to capitalize on past research results. The reason for this is that results are summarized in static tables and figures that only barely capture the underlying data, the code that is used to implement a method, or the detail evaluation results. As a result, data, code and detailed evaluation results are often not made available in the relevant publication. OpenML (Open Machine Learning) aims to change the way we perform machine learning research so that it becomes more in line with a network science paradigm. The main idea behind OpenML is that of creating an information space for machine learning research where datasets, workflows (flows) and research results (runs) can be made available in an automated fashion. This should take little more than clicking a few buttons in data mining tools that we are used to such as Weka, R, MOA and RapidMiner. In the OpenML framework we create designed serendipity and achieve a dynamic division of labor that in practice helps us in solving many of the hard problems that we still have, in performing large-scale meta-research studies, and in facilitating an automatic competition for new models with all other models in existence. As a byproduct, OpenML benefits also individual researchers, their students, and practitioners by providing more visibility for their work, with new impact measures, and with what we may call a working memory of a scientific field.
